runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Congressional Budget Office (2012) - Labor Supply Elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit lower substitution elasticities compared to secondary earners. For mid-to-high income earners (decile 7), response is typically constrained by institutional factors like fixed work hours, though some intensive margin adjustment and reporting responses exist.

  "interpretation": "Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 7 in a U.S. microsimulation model",

For mid-to-high income earners (decile 7), response is typically constrained by institutional factors like fixed work hours, though some intensive margin adjustment and reporting responses exist."

citations: Congressional Budget Office (CBO) labor supply assumptions
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners generally exhibit low substitution elasticities compared to secondary earners. For mid-to-high earners (decile 7), the response is constrained by institutional work requirements and full-time norms, though slightly higher than the bottom deciles due to lower participation margins and higher intensive margin salience. runs.interpretation: The primary-earner substitution elasticity for U.S. households in earnings decile 7, representing the percentage change in hours or earnings relative to a percentage change in the net-of-tax marginal wage rate.
citations: CBO (2012) - How the Supply of Labor Responds to Changes in After-Tax Wages
Chetty (2012) - Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) - The Elasticity of Taxable Income with Respect to Marginal Tax Rates
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners exhibit lower substitution elasticities than secondary earners. In the 7th decile, earners are generally full-time and have fewer degrees of freedom for immediate labor supply adjustment than lower-income earners (who may be in and out of the labor force) or very high earners (who have more control over the timing and form of compensation), leading to a relatively inelastic point estimate.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "The primary-earner substitution elasticity for U.S. households in earnings decile 7, representing the percentage change in hours or earnings relative to a percentage change in the net-of-tax marginal wage rate.",
  "point_estimate": 0.15,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.05,
    "p25": 0.1,
    "p50": 0.15,
    "p75": 0.25,
    "p95": 0.4
  },
  "citations": [
    "CBO (2012) - How the Supply of Labor Responds to Changes in After-Tax Wages",
    "Chetty (2012) - Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions",
    "Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) - The Elasticity of Taxable Income with Respect to Marginal Tax Rates"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Primary earners exhibit lower substitution elasticities than secondary earners. In the 7th decile, earners are generally full-time and have fewer degrees of freedom for immediate labor supply adjustment than lower-income earners (who may be in and out of the labor force) or very high earners (who have more control over the timing and form of compensation), leading to a relatively inelastic point estimate."
}

runs.interpretation: Substitution elasticity for primary earners in the 7th decile of U.S. earnings used in tax-benefit microsimulation models.
citations: CBO (Congressional Budget Office) labor supply reports
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners typically exhibit lower labor supply elasticity than secondary earners. In the middle-to-upper deciles (decile 7), hours are relatively inelastic, and the substitution effect is generally estimated as small but positive, reflecting intensive margin adjustments. Microsimulation models like those used by the CBO or Tax Policy Center typically use values in the 0.1 to 0.2 range for this population.

  "interpretation": "Substitution elasticity for primary earners in the 7th decile of U.S. earnings used in tax-benefit microsimulation models.",

    "p25": 0.05,
    "p50": 0.1,

  },

    "CBO (Congressional Budget Office) labor supply reports",
    "Saez (2001) optimal tax calibrations",

  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Primary earners typically exhibit lower labor supply elasticity than secondary earners. In the middle-to-upper deciles (decile 7), hours are relatively inelastic, and the substitution effect is generally estimated as small but positive, reflecting intensive margin adjustments. Microsimulation models like those used by the CBO or Tax Policy Center typically use values in the 0.1 to 0.2 range for this population."
}

}
